era_context: The early 15th century continues with the Ming Dynasty under the Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435), the Ottoman Empire consolidating power, the Italian Renaissance in its early flowering, and the Hussite Wars ongoing in Central Europe. The Council of Constance is in its final years. Portuguese exploration of the African coast is beginning. The Aztec Empire is consolidating control over central Mexico.

events.title: Joan of Arc executed; end of her military campaigns
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French peasant girl who claimed divine inspiration and led French military campaigns during the Hundred Years' War, is executed by burning in Rouen on 30 May 1431. Joan had been captured by Burgundian forces in 1430 and handed over to the English. She was tried for heresy and witchcraft by an ecclesiastical court dominated by English interests. Her execution marks the end of her military campaigns, which had begun in 1429 and had achieved significant victories, including the relief of Orléans and the coronation of Charles VII at Reims. Joan's death does not end the Hundred Years' War, but it marks a symbolic turning point: the French, inspired by Joan's legacy, continue to fight and eventually expel the English from France by 1453. Joan's execution is one of the most famous events of the medieval period and has become a symbol of French nationalism and religious martyrdom.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (French military leader, executed)
Charles VII (King of France, beneficiary of Joan's campaigns)
Henry VI (King of England, nominal beneficiary of Joan's capture)
Pierre Cauchon (Bishop of Beauvais, presiding judge)

sources.name: Trial records of Joan of Arc (contemporary primary source, official trial documentation)
Jean de Metz and Jean de Belleville, contemporary accounts of Joan's military campaigns
Chroniques of Jean Froissart and other contemporary chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Joan of Arc's execution is one of the best-documented events of the medieval period. The date (30 May 1431) is firmly established in trial records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Council of Constance: final sessions and ecclesiastical reform
events.description: The Council of Constance, which began in 1414, continues its final sessions in 1431. The council has already condemned Jan Hus (1415) and Jerome of Prague (1416) for heresy and has worked to heal the Western Schism. In 1431, the council is addressing remaining ecclesiastical reform issues and preparing for its formal conclusion in 1434. The council's work represents a major reassertion of papal authority and an attempt to address corruption and schism within the Catholic Church.

sources.name: Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronik des Konstanzer Konzils (contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance's sessions are extensively documented. The 1431 date is confirmed in official records.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet: fifth major voyage begins
events.description: Zheng He's treasure fleet departs on its fifth major voyage (1431–1433), which will explore the Indian Ocean, reaching East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The fleet comprises hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel. The voyage demonstrates Ming China's technological superiority in naval construction and navigation. However, the expeditions are increasingly opposed by Confucian officials at court, who view them as wasteful. This voyage will be followed by a sixth voyage (1433–1435) and a seventh and final voyage (1433–1434), after which the Ming court will cease to fund large-scale maritime expeditions.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, patron of the voyage)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The dates and scale of Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming court records. The fifth voyage is dated to 1431–1433 in official sources.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Taborite military campaigns continue
events.description: The Hussite Wars continue in 1431, with the radical Taborite faction conducting military campaigns against Catholic forces and moderate Hussite opponents. The Taborites, inspired by the legacy of Jan Hus and John Žižka, advocate radical social and religious reform. In 1431, the Taborites are at the height of their military power, though they face increasing opposition from a coalition of moderate Hussites (Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The year 1431 represents the final phase of radical Hussite dominance; the Battle of Lipany in 1434 will decisively defeat the Taborites.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Hussite Chronicle (contemporary Hussite account)

events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars are well-documented, but specific military campaigns within 1431 are not always precisely dated. The general context of Taborite military activity is confirmed.

events.title: Council of Constance convenes Council of Siena; ecclesiastical continuity
events.description: As the Council of Constance prepares to conclude, ecclesiastical authorities convene the Council of Siena (1431–1449) to continue the work of ecclesiastical reform and to address ongoing religious controversies. The Council of Siena is called to address the Hussite question and to continue the reform agenda begun at Constance. The council represents the continuity of ecclesiastical authority and the determination of the Catholic Church to address heresy and reform. However, the Council of Siena is less significant than the Council of Constance and is often overshadowed by it in historical narratives.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (successor to Martin V, reigning 1431–1447)
events.certainty_note: The Council of Siena is documented in official ecclesiastical records. The 1431 date is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's trial records, while extensive, are not entirely reliable: they were compiled by English-dominated ecclesiastical authorities with a vested interest in her conviction. Modern scholarship has questioned some details of her trial, particularly regarding the charges of witchcraft and the extent to which she was coerced into confessing. Some historians argue that Joan's military significance has been exaggerated by later French nationalist historiography; while her campaigns were important, they were not decisive in the Hundred Years' War, which was ultimately decided by broader political and military factors. The Council of Constance's significance in healing the Western Schism is sometimes overstated; the schism had already begun to heal before the council convened, and the council's role was more to formalize and consolidate existing trends. The Council of Siena is often overlooked in historical narratives, but it played an important role in continuing ecclesiastical reform.
historiographic_note: The year 1431 is well-documented for Europe, particularly France and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Joan of Arc's trial records are among the most detailed documents of the medieval period. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The historical record for 1431 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia, with Joan of Arc's execution dominating Western historical narratives.

graph_edges.note: Joan of Arc's execution in 1431 marks the end of her military campaigns, which began in 1429.
Joan of Arc's execution in 1431 inspires French resistance, which ultimately leads to the end of the Hundred Years' War in 1453.
The Council of Constance's work in 1431 leads to its formal conclusion in 1434.
Zheng He's fifth voyage (1431–1433) is part of the series of treasure fleet expeditions that culminate in his final voyage in 1434.
Hussite military campaigns in 1431 lead to the decisive Battle of Lipany in 1434.
The Council of Siena, convened in 1431, continues until 1449.
